_comment: Parent notes shown on cards/pages. Every claim must be verifiable against the park's official City of Boise description or a cited city page. Keys are park slugs.
ann-morrison-park: Boise's big classic park. The interactive fountain (Memorial Day–Labor Day, sunrise to sunset) is a summer ritual — bring towels and a change of clothes. The playground has bonded rubber surfacing, and the huge lawns absorb any amount of energy.
julia-davis-park: You can stack a whole day here: Zoo Boise, the Rose Garden, the bandshell and the Boise River all live inside the park, with the best mature shade in town. Year-round restrooms make it a reliable winter outing too.
esther-simplot-park: The swim-friendly ponds with gentle, beach-style edges are the summer draw — pack water shoes and sunscreen, shade is still filling in. No pets in the park or ponds, which many parents appreciate. Quinn's Pond and the whitewater park are next door.
camels-back-park: North End favorite: a playground with bonded rubber surfacing under mature trees, tennis courts, and the famous hill — kids climb it in summer and sled it after a good snow. Hyde Park's treats are a block away. Heated restrooms stay open all year.
kathryn-albertson-park: No playground here — this one is for wildlife walks. Wide paved loops are perfect for strollers and new walkers, and ducks and other waterfowl are basically guaranteed. Swimming, wading, biking and scooters are not allowed; it stays calm.
veterans-memorial-park: A brand-new playground went in for 2025. Add the pond, open lawns, Greenbelt access and year-round restrooms and it's one of the most complete stops on the west side of downtown.
kristin-armstrong-municipal-park: A shady 28 acres on the Boise River near the East End — reservable picnic areas make it a birthday-party staple, and heated restrooms are open year-round.
ivywild-park: Home of Ivywild Pool, an outdoor pool with a toddler pool and slides (seasonal, admission applies). The park around it has a playground, tennis courts and sports fields.
borah-park: A summer trifecta: outdoor pool, drop-in spray pad (10 a.m.–8 p.m., Memorial Day–Labor Day) and a playground, plus a community garden and picnic shelters.
comba-park: Small but mighty: a real splash pad (10 a.m.–8 p.m., Memorial Day–Labor Day), a playground and the BUGS teaching garden. Heads up — there are no restrooms at this park.
molenaar-park: One of West Boise's most complete newer parks: splash pad, 2021 playground, skate park, pond, walking loop and a fully fenced dog off-leash area so the family dog gets a turn too.
terry-day-park: A Federal Way favorite: bonded-rubber playground, a fishing pond, picnic shelter, tennis courts, two parking lots and a heated year-round restroom.
bowler-park: A newer (2021) playground plus button-activated misters for hot days, a fenced dog off-leash area, disc golf and year-round restrooms in Southeast Boise near Surprise Valley.
hobble-creek-park: West Boise workhorse next to the West YMCA: a 2020 playground, a pickleball complex, a 0.58-mile loop path ideal for scooters and balance bikes, and three covered shelters.
cassia-park: The 2020 playground includes a wheelchair-accessible We-Go-Round spinner on bonded rubber surfacing — one of the Bench's best accessible playgrounds, with a walking path and shady picnic spots.
hyatt-hidden-lakes-reserve: A wetland reserve rather than a playground park — think bird spotting from the overlooks and an easy walk on the paths. Year-round restrooms at the trailhead. No swimming, wading or bikes.
marianne-williams-park: Big open riverside park in Barber Valley with two large ponds and flat paved paths that are perfect for strollers. No playground yet, and bikes aren't allowed in the interior — it's a walking park.
simplot-sports-complex: Tournament central. If you're here for a sibling's game, there's a 2018 playground with bonded rubber surfacing to keep the younger crew busy.
fort-boise-park: More rec hub than playground park — an in-ground skate park, ballfields and six tennis courts alongside the Fort Boise Community Center. No playground.
grove-plaza: Downtown's interactive fountain (8 a.m.–11 p.m., Memorial Day–Labor Day) is a classic run-through-the-jets stop after the library or Saturday market. No restrooms or playground — it's a plaza.
pioneer-tot-lot: A tiny downtown pocket playground in the River Street neighborhood — handy for a quick energy burn, but no restrooms.
manitou-park: Classic Southeast Boise neighborhood park: mature shade trees, big open lawn, playground and a first-come picnic shelter.
winstead-park: Popular Bench park with a walking path, tennis courts and playground under established trees — sports teams use the open fields most evenings.
sunset-park: Old-school Bench park with mature shade, a playground, ballfield and a first-come, first-served picnic shelter.
redwood-park: West Boise favorite with a fishing pond, walking path, playground and year-round restrooms. Off-leash dog hours run during designated times, so expect dogs early and late.
aldape-park: A 0.4-acre pocket lawn under mature trees in East Boise — fine for a blanket picnic, but there's no playground, restroom or parking.
baggley-park: Southeast Boise all-rounder with Greenbelt access: an all-ages playground, tennis, basketball, sand volleyball and a shelter. Restrooms are seasonal with a winter portable.
barber-observation-point: Not a play park — a short walking path, interpretive signs and a big view over the Barber Pool Conservation Area. Has its own small lot; no restrooms.
bernardine-quinn-riverside-park: Home of Quinn's Pond, where fishing docks and open water draw swimmers and paddleboarders, off the Whitewater Park Blvd lot. No pets, and no restrooms on this side — use Esther Simplot Park next door.
boise-hills-park: Foothills-edge park with a bonded-rubber playground, a natural play area and the Kristin Armstrong Children's Bike Trail, plus tennis, pickleball and basketball.
bowden-park: Next to South Junior High: a 2022 playground with an inclusive Avalanche slide and Ten Spin spinner on bonded rubber, plus an outdoor gym for the grown-ups.
c-w-moore-park: A downtown pocket park of benches and historical artifacts — a shady snack stop on a stroller walk, with a year-round restroom that's rare for downtown.
camels-back-reserve: The foothills trails rising behind Camel's Back Park — combine a playground stop with a first 'real hike'. No facilities up top; use the park below.
castle-hills-park: Northwest Boise sleeper: the playground was rebuilt in 2025 on bonded rubber, joined by tennis, pickleball, basketball, practice fields and an hourly dog off-leash area.
catalpa-park: Full classic-park kit — all-ages playground, shelter, sand volleyball, horseshoes, tennis and an informal skateboard area — with a small lot on the south edge.
cecil-d-andrus-park: Shady brick-path square across from the State Capitol — a good sandwich stop after the museum or library, not a play destination.
charles-f-mcdevitt-youth-sports-complex: Little League central at Eagle and McMillan, with a brand-new 2026 playground, a fishing pond and a skate park to fill time between siblings' innings.
cherie-buckner-webb-park: Downtown's newest park, built where a parking lot used to be — small, central, and with a heated year-round restroom.
cottonwood-park: West Boise neighborhood stop with a 2019 playground on bonded rubber (Ten Spin spinner) and a year-round restroom — a reliable winter option.
cypress-park: Southeast Boise park with an all-ages playground, picnic shelter, walking path, tennis and a genuinely nice view of the Boise Front.
demeyer-park: Pond-side West Boise park: all-ages playground, volleyball, basketball and a conservation station for curious kids. Street parking.
eagle-rock-park: East End park near the Old Penitentiary with an all-ages playground, reservable shelter and courts — a solid backup when the river parks are packed.
elm-grove-park: North End classic under big trees: all-ages playground, tennis, basketball and a grass softball field.
fairmont-park: Pool-plus-playground combo next to Morley Nelson Elementary — the municipal pool is the summer draw, and the shelter is first-come, first-served.
fairview-park: Small but stacked: a 2025 playground with a spinner and net climber on bonded rubber, plus button-activated misters for hot days.
florence-park: A simple open-lawn park with a playground and a small pull-in lot — low-key by design.
foothills-east-park: A natural-area gateway into the foothills trails rather than a play park — no facilities.
franklin-park: Central Bench standout: bonded-rubber 2022 playground, misters, a quarter-mile paved loop, outdoor gym, bocce courts and a year-round restroom.
gordon-s-bowen-park: Tiny North End pocket with a playground, drinking fountain and bike racks — a quick stop, not an outing.
helen-b-lowder-park: Next to Liberty Elementary: playground, tennis, basketball and sand volleyball, with a small lot by the courts.
hewett-park: Quiet West Boise park with two playground structures rebuilt in 2024 on bonded rubber, reached through neighborhood streets — street parking only.
hillside-park: A ballfield park below the foothills: a lighted softball diamond, youth diamonds and even a spot for horses. It's a game-day place — no playground here.
hillside-to-hollow-reserve: Easy foothills sagebrush walking off Bogus Basin Rd with a year-round restroom at the trailhead — the small lots fill early on weekends.
hulls-gulch-reserve: The classic close-in foothills hike from the 8th St trailheads — bring water and sun protection; no facilities once you're on the trail.
idaho-anne-frank-human-rights-memorial: A quiet, moving educational memorial along the Greenbelt — best for older kids, and easy to pair with the library next door.
idaho-fallen-firefighter-memorial: Shady Greenbelt lawn west of downtown with memorials honoring fallen firefighters and 9/11 — a reflective picnic stop with seasonal restrooms.
ja-kaff-boise-whitewater-park: Watch kayakers and river surfers ride the adjustable wave — grade-schoolers find it mesmerizing. Park in the Esther Simplot / Quinn's Pond lots.
jullion-park: Leafy southwest-Boise neighborhood park: all-ages playground, non-reservable shelter and plenty of open lawn.
kaixo-corner: A tiny plaza on the Basque Block celebrating the neighborhood's immigrant history — a cultural pause on a downtown walk, not a play stop.
kristens-park: A tiny unmarked green space on W River St — no facilities.
kroeger-park: Half-acre pocket park off Broadway with shade trees, picnic tables and a surprisingly complete playground — slides, swings and a zipline-style track ride.
laura-moore-cunningham-arboretum: A working city tree nursery in the East End rather than a destination park.
liberty-park: Emerald St neighborhood park by St. Al's, with a community garden, a first-come covered shelter, youth ball diamonds and a playground.
magnolia-park: Sunrise-to-sunset park off Bogart Ln with fishing, a shelter, open lawns and its own parking lot (per the city).
mariposa-park: A nature-walk park: pond, paths, a pollinator garden with monarch-butterfly signage and a fenced dog area — but no playground.
mcauley-park: Benches under big old trees in the North End — a shady pause, nothing more.
memorial-park: Simple northeast-Boise lawn with an all-ages playground and seasonal restrooms.
military-reserve: 734 acres of close-in trails plus the Albertson family bike park (with beginner lines), an archery range and a dog off-leash area — big-kid territory.
milwaukee-park: Five Little League fields next to Capital High — a game-day park with no playground.
morris-hill-park: Bench favorite: bonded-rubber playground, picnic shelter, horseshoes, an arboretum and a fenced dog park with a separate small-dog side. Heated restroom open year-round.
mountain-view-park: West Boise basics done well: a toddler-focused playground (ages 2–5), tennis, basketball and picnic tables. Street parking.
nottingham-park: Two-acre mini park with a full playground — net climber, slides, swings — and a basketball court.
optimist-youth-sports-complex: Weekend-soccer headquarters in northwest Boise, with a fishing pond and public-art pieces to wander past. Note the seasonal pet restrictions — and there's no playground.
oregon-trail-reserve: Walk a real piece of the Oregon Trail down the historic Kelton Ramp — short and genuinely cool for school-age kids. Trailhead lot and a year-round restroom.
owens-park: Quiet picnic-table pocket park in the Sycamore/Collister neighborhood — no facilities.
owyhee-park: Bench park near Vista with playground equipment for both little kids and big kids, four tennis courts and practice fields.
parkcenter-park: Pond park in East Boise that fills with waterfowl in spring and summer — bring binoculars. Year-round restroom; street parking.
peppermint-park: Next to Pepper Ridge Elementary: a bonded-rubber playground, first-come shelter and tennis, pickleball and basketball courts.
phillippi-park: One of the Bench's best accessible playgrounds — universally accessible design with a Ten Spin on bonded rubber (2020) — plus a reservable shelter, courts and an hourly dog area.
pine-grove-park: A 2023 bonded-rubber playground with an inclusive swing seat, two shelters, half-court hoops and a fenced dog park.
platt-gardens: The landscaped gardens in front of the historic Boise Depot — train-curious kids can peek at the depot itself. No pets on the grounds.
polecat-gulch-reserve: Foothills loop trails between Cartwright and Collister with a trailhead lot off Cartwright Rd — sun-exposed, so aim for mornings.
primrose-park: Small but thoughtfully rebuilt in 2024 — the playground has accessible features, and there's an outdoor gym, a pollinator garden, a shelter and van-accessible ADA parking.
rhodes-park: Downtown's skate park, designed with input from local youth — great for skater kids and mesmerizing for younger spectators. Seasonal restroom.
rotary-park-on-the-greenbelt: A tiny Greenbelt rest stop — a bench and a river view, no facilities.
shoreline-park: Shady Greenbelt lawn with a river overlook and — unusually for a small park — a year-round restroom. A good mid-walk pit stop.
shoshone-park: Vista-area neighborhood park: playground, two tennis courts, practice fields and a first-come, first-served shelter.
skyline-park: Mini West Bench park with a playground, basketball hoops and a first-come shelter.
stewart-gulch-park: Small foothills-side park with a bonded-rubber playground and covered shelter — sun-exposed, so mornings are best.
sycamore-park: West Boise park whose playground includes a zipline-style track ride, plus public art and a non-reservable shelter. Lot on the school side.
warm-springs-park: Riverside open space in the East End with Greenbelt access, birding and a native plant garden — plus a year-round restroom. More nature ramble than playground (there isn't one).
williams-park: Southeast Boise park built around four tennis courts, with a playground, looping paths and an off-leash zone for the dog.
willow-lane-athletic-complex: Softball and soccer complex on the Greenbelt with a small ages-2–5 playground and a dirt-jump bike park. Note the seasonal pet rules.
willow-lane-park: Compact northwest park with a merry-go-round-equipped playground, basketball and pickleball — it shares the athletic complex lot next door.
